learning_outcomes.description: Apply specific marketing and distribution theories and concepts applicable to the tourism, hospitality and event industries.
Collect and analyse market research data relevant for tourism, hospitality and event organisations to choose appropriate distribution channels.
Employ problem-solving techniques to design a marketing report for usage by a tourism, hospitality or event organisation.
As a group, develop presentation skills for an organization in the tourism, hospitality or events industries.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment is designed to help you better utilise existing studies to formulate a marketing report. You are required to first choose a THE-related organisation located in Australia. You must then identify four peer-reviewed journal articles relevant to your organisation, specifically addressing: (1) market segmentation and (2) distribution channels.

The insights gained from these four journal articles should help you understand the business context, industry competition, customer niche markets, and distribution channels relevant to your chosen organisation. You are required to critically review these four articles (published after 2020), provide a systematic analysis, and incorporate and reflect on their recommendations in relation to your selected organisation.

The key objective of this assessment is to learn from published research and leverage existing knowledge to design an effective and evidence-based marketing strategy for the organisation.

Please note that the chosen organisation will be used across Assessments 1, 2, and 3. Assessments 1 and 2 are individual tasks, while Assessment 3 is team-based. You are therefore required to select the organisation in consultation with your team members to ensure that all members agree on the chosen organisation as the case study.

Output

A 2000-words essay
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit to Turnitin through Blackboard Assessment link.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment aims to develop your ability to conduct a competitive analysis within the tourism industry by evaluating the positioning, branding, and strategic advantages of a chosen company relative to its competitors. The task is to conduct a competitive analysis of the competitors to the company you have chosen. At least five competitors have to be included. As an example, if your choose W Hotel as the main case, five competitors could be Marriorit, Hyatt, Pullman, Emporium, and Calile Hotel.

You need to produce a poster that includes the following information:

  • Identify your company and the competitors
  • Conduct an environmental analysis of your company in comparison to the strengths and weaknesses.
  • Create a position map for your company in relation to other competitors.
  • From here, identify how your company should be positioned and branded in relation to its competitors 

Output

A poster in PDF format.

(Detailed instructions on poster preparation along with references will be available on BB)

The assessment will be unpacked during week 1 and we will have a poster preparation presentation on week 7.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit to Turnitin through Blackboard Assessment link.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Marketing Research - Part A - Video Presentation
assessments.task_description:
The assessment requires you to develop a comprehensive marketing report addressing the chosen organisation’s: (1) target segments, (2) distribution channels, (3) competition, and (4) sustainability performance. It requires a comprehensive understanding of your organisation and the THE industry more broadly.

Assessment 3 consists of two components.

  • Part A is a recorded group presentation
  • Part B is an interactive oral assessment, in the form of a live Q&A session with your lecturer, which will take place during the live seminar in week 12: Monday 19th of October 8 am- 11 am.

Further details will be provided on Blackboard.

Output

Part A: Your team needs to submit a recorded presentation with a length of 10 minutes. Please do not exceed 12 minutes as this will make the file too big and take up the server space (UQ suggestion). The recorded file needs to include

  • PPT slides
  • The presenter(s) – each member of the team needs to present

Peer Evaluation

A peer evaluation in group assessment allows team members to provide feedback on each other's contributions, collaboration skills, and work quality, helping instructors gain insight into individual performance within the group dynamic and ensuring more equitable grading.

This Part A of assessment is secured via Part B's Live Oral Q&A.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Marketing Research - Part B - Live Interactive Oral Q&A
assessments.due_date:
Week 12, Mon

During the live seminar. From 8 am - 11 am AEST.
assessments.task_description:
Part B is an live 10 minute interactive oral assessment, in the form of a live Q&A session with your lecturer and facilitator, which will take place during the live seminar in week 12: 19th of October 8 am- 11 am.

Everyone must attend this day.

You'll be given a question bank to study prior to the Q&A session.

Please Note: Part B will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Overview

Overview of the subject, covering key concepts including what is marketing, what is a product, and what is marketing research, within the context of the tourism and hospitality industry. Students will be introduced to the three assessments and their logistics, and will form their teams for Assessment 3.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar - Overview Overview of the subject, covering key concepts including what is marketing, what is a product, and what is marketing research, within the context of the tourism and hospitality industry. Students will be introduced to the three assessments and their logistics, and will form their teams for Assessment 3.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Target market segmentation

Market segmentation is a strategic process that involves dividing a broad target market into smaller, more defined groups of consumers who share similar characteristics, needs, or behaviours. This approach allows TH&E businesses to tailor their products, services, and marketing efforts more effectively to meet the specific needs of each segment. Students will be introduced to geographic, demographic, psychographic and behavioural segmentation approaches, and will conduct an in-class article search to identify relevant journal and industry sources in preparation for Assessment 1.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar - Target market segmentation Market segmentation is a strategic process that involves dividing a broad target market into smaller, more defined groups of consumers who share similar characteristics, needs, or behaviours. This approach allows TH&E businesses to tailor their products, services, and marketing efforts more effectively to meet the specific needs of each segment. Students will be introduced to geographic, demographic, psychographic and behavioural segmentation approaches, and will conduct an in-class article search to identify relevant journal and industry sources in preparation for Assessment 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar- Distribution channels

In the tourism and hospitality industry, effective distribution channels are essential for connecting service providers.

Such as hotels, airlines, tour operators, and attractions, with consumers. These channels facilitate the promotion, booking, and delivery of tourism products and experiences. Students will explore direct and indirect distribution channels through a case study illustrating their function and importance, and will conduct a source hunt to identify relevant articles in support of Assessment 1. The concept of OTA's will also be introduced to support the distribution channel analysis.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar- Distribution channels In the tourism and hospitality industry, effective distribution channels are essential for connecting service providers. Such as hotels, airlines, tour operators, and attractions, with consumers. These channels facilitate the promotion, booking, and delivery of tourism products and experiences. Students will explore direct and indirect distribution channels through a case study illustrating their function and importance, and will conduct a source hunt to identify relevant articles in support of Assessment 1. The concept of OTA's will also be introduced to support the distribution channel analysis.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Segmentation strategies and consumer behaviour

Building on the concepts introduced in market segmentation and distribution channels, this week brings consumer behaviour into focus. Students will explore how an understanding of consumer behaviour, preferences, and tourism activities can deepen their analysis of target markets. Using data from the Tourism Research Australia (TRA) database, students will engage in practical exercises examining how quantitative data can be used to identify and interpret target market behaviour, strengthening their analytical foundations.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar - Segmentation strategies and consumer behaviour Building on the concepts introduced in market segmentation and distribution channels, this week brings consumer behaviour into focus. Students will explore how an understanding of consumer behaviour, preferences, and tourism activities can deepen their analysis of target markets. Using data from the Tourism Research Australia (TRA) database, students will engage in practical exercises examining how quantitative data can be used to identify and interpret target market behaviour, strengthening their analytical foundations.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Holistic market segmentation analysis

This week expands students' understanding of digital distribution by exploring the role of Online Travel Agencies (OTAs) and Search Engine Optimisation (SEO) in tourism marketing. OTAs provide global visibility and access to large customer bases, and students will examine how they influence consumer decision-making through reviews, rankings, and dynamic pricing, positioning them as powerful intermediaries in the tourism market. A case study using social media data to perform a market segmentation analysis of the Brisbane Festival will allow students to apply these concepts in a local industry context.

Last Q&A for assessment 1 which is due this week: Wednesday 26 August, 1:00 PM

Last Q&A for assessment 1 which is due this week:  Wednesday 26 August, 1:00 PM

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar- Analysis framework

Competition analysis is a critical component of strategic planning that enables businesses to understand their competitive landscape, identify opportunities and threats, and position themselves effectively in the market. This week looks at 2 analytical frameworks: SWOT analysis, and the BCG Matrix. Students will explore how each tool can be applied to evaluate an organisation's competitive position and product portfolio within the tourism and hospitality industry.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar- Analysis framework Competition analysis is a critical component of strategic planning that enables businesses to understand their competitive landscape, identify opportunities and threats, and position themselves effectively in the market. This week looks at 2 analytical frameworks: SWOT analysis, and the BCG Matrix. Students will explore how each tool can be applied to evaluate an organisation's competitive position and product portfolio within the tourism and hospitality industry.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Competition analysis

Positioning is a fundamental concept in marketing strategy that defines how a brand or organisation is perceived relative to its competitors in the minds of consumers. This week introduces the positioning map. Students will explore how to select meaningful axes, interpret competitive clusters, and identify gaps in the market.

Through a guided exercise, students will apply the positioning map to analyse the competitive positioning of a chosen tourism or hospitality organisation against its key competitors.

The second part of the seminar introduces the practical elements of poster design, guiding students through structure, layout, and visual communication principles in preparation for Assessment 2
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar - Competition analysis Positioning is a fundamental concept in marketing strategy that defines how a brand or organisation is perceived relative to its competitors in the minds of consumers. This week introduces the positioning map. Students will explore how to select meaningful axes, interpret competitive clusters, and identify gaps in the market. Through a guided exercise, students will apply the positioning map to analyse the competitive positioning of a chosen tourism or hospitality organisation against its key competitors. The second part of the seminar introduces the practical elements of poster design, guiding students through structure, layout, and visual communication principles in preparation for Assessment 2

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - IMC and marketing mix

This week covers the marketing mix and Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC), focusing on the importance of aligning message and media strategies to deliver a consistent marketing message. Students will examine how rational and emotional appeals are used in message design, and will compare major media options including advertising, public relations, personal selling, and digital marketing. The growing role of digital marketing in tourism communication is also explored.

Q&A session for A2 due week 9.
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar - IMC and marketing mix This week covers the marketing mix and Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC), focusing on the importance of aligning message and media strategies to deliver a consistent marketing message. Students will examine how rational and emotional appeals are used in message design, and will compare major media options including advertising, public relations, personal selling, and digital marketing. The growing role of digital marketing in tourism communication is also explored. Q&A session for A2 due week 9.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Marketing mix (II)

Marketing research and campaign development are explored this week through the concept of the media mix, which refers to the blend of different digital and traditional media channels used to communicate a brand's message. Students will examine how the media mix can be tailored based on target market, campaign objectives, budget, and desired outcomes, moving away from a one-size-fits-all approach. A case study exploring celebrity marketing will be used.

Assessment 2 is due this week on Wednesday 23 September at 1:00 PM.

Students will examine how the media mix can be tailored based on target market, campaign objectives, budget, and desired outcomes, moving away from a one-size-fits-all approach. A case study exploring celebrity marketing will be used. Assessment 2 is due this week on Wednesday 23 September at 1:00 PM.

learning_activities.topic:
Assertive communication: Guest lecture (TBC)

Due to the King's birthday public holiday, the Monday face-to-face seminar session will not take place this week. In its place, students will learn from a guest lecturer focusing on assertive communication skills.

This session is designed to help students strengthen their verbal communication, confidence, and clarity in preparation for the live Q&A component of Assessment 3.

The seminar will be rescheduled for week 10. More details on the new date will be discussed in week 8 and 9, and posted on Blackboard.

The second section of the seminar will review the overview of logistics for the live Q&A. Part B of assessment 3.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Sustainability and CSR

This week examines interconnected themes shaping responsible business practice in the tourism and hospitality industry. Students will explore sustainability as a strategic imperative, examining how organisations can integrate environmental and social considerations into their marketing and operations.

Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is introduced as a framework through which businesses communicate their commitment to ethical practices and community impact. The week also addresses greenwashing.

Last Q&A session for A3.

Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is introduced as a framework through which businesses communicate their commitment to ethical practices and community impact. The week also addresses greenwashing. Last Q&A session for A3.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar - Live Q&A. Part B of A3.

This week is dedicated to the live Interactive Oral Assessment for Assessment 3 Part B. Each team will participate in a face-to-face Q&A session with the teaching team, responding to questions drawn from the question bank provided in advance. Students are expected to demonstrate a thorough understanding of their chosen organisation, their research findings, and the analytical frameworks applied throughout the semester. All team members must attend their allocated session time. A schedule will be provided in advance.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar- Reflections and Marketing yourself

This week students reflect on their Q&A experience.

The seminar also explores the concept of personal marketing, examining how the principles covered throughout the semester can be applied to individual professional development, personal branding, and employability in the tourism and hospitality industry.
